Minimum Slack Priority Rule (MSP):
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Compute earliest start times (EST) and latest start times (LST) for all jobs using forward and backward passes without resource constraints
Step 3: Calculate slack time for each job as Slack = LST - EST
Step 4: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 5: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 6: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 7: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with minimum slack time
Step 8: If multiple jobs have equal minimum slack, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 9: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 10: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 11: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 12: Repeat steps 5-11 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 13: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 14: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 15: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 16: Repeat steps 5-15 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 17: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
Greatest Resource Demand Priority Rule (:
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 3: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 4: Calculate resource demand for each eligible job (typically: resource requirement × duration or sum across all resources)
Step 5: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 6: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with greatest resource demand
Step 7: If multiple jobs have equal greatest resource demand, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 8: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 9: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 10: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 11: Repeat steps 3-10 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 12: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 13: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 14: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 15: Repeat steps 3-14 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 16: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
Most Successors Priority Rule (MSS):
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Compute successor count for each job by analyzing the precedence network (direct and indirect successors)
Step 3: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 4: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 5: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 6: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with the most successors
Step 7: If multiple jobs have equal most successors, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 8: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 9: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 10: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 11: Repeat steps 4-10 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 12: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 13: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 14: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 15: Repeat steps 4-14 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 16: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs

Earliest Start Time Priority Rule (EST):
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Compute earliest start times (EST) for all jobs using forward pass without resource constraints
Step 3: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 4: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 5: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 6: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with earliest start time (minimum EST)
Step 7: If multiple jobs have equal earliest start time, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 8: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 9: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 10: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 11: Repeat steps 4-10 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 12: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 13: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 14: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 15: Repeat steps 4-14 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 16: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
Earliest Finish Time Priority Rule (EFT):
Step1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step2: Compute earliest finish times (EFT) for all jobs using forward pass without resource constraints
Step3: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step4: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step5: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step6: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with earliest finish time (minimum EFT)
Step7: If multiple jobs have equal earliest finish time, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step8: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step9: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step10: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step11: Repeat steps 4-10 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step12: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step13: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step14: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step15: Repeat steps 4-14 until all jobs are scheduled
Step16: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
Latest Start Time Priority Rule (LST):
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Compute earliest start times (EST) and earliest finish times (EFT) for all jobs using forward pass without resource constraints
Step 3: Compute latest start times (LST) for all jobs using backward pass with project deadline set to critical path length
Step 4: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 5: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 6: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 7: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with minimum latest start time (LST)
Step 8: If multiple jobs have equal minimum LST, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 9: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 10: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 11: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 12: Repeat steps 5-11 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 13: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 14: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 15: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 16: Repeat steps 5-15 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 17: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs

Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Compute earliest start times (EST) and earliest finish times (EFT) for all jobs using forward pass without resource constraints
Step 3: Compute latest finish times (LFT) for all jobs using backward pass with project deadline set to critical path length
Step 4: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 5: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 6: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 7: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with minimum latest finish time (LFT)
Step 8: If multiple jobs have equal minimum LFT, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 9: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 10: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 11: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 12: Repeat steps 5-11 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 13: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 14: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 15: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 16: Repeat steps 5-15 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 17: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
Greatest Rank Positional Weight Priority:
Step 1: Initialize parameters including job durations, resource requirements, resource capacities, and precedence relationships
Step 2: Calculate positional weight for each job as sum of its duration plus durations of all its successors (direct and indirect) in precedence network
Step 3: Initialize current time t = 0, scheduled job list = empty, and active job list = empty
Step 4: Identify eligible jobs (all predecessors completed and not yet scheduled)
Step 5: Among eligible jobs, select those with sufficient resources available at current time
Step 6: From resource-feasible eligible jobs, select job with greatest positional weight (GRPW)
Step 7: If multiple jobs have equal greatest positional weight, apply tie-breaking rule (e.g., shortest processing time)
Step 8: Schedule selected job at current time with its required resources
Step 9: Update resource availability by subtracting resources allocated to newly scheduled job
Step 10: Add scheduled job to active job list with completion time = t + duration
Step 11: Repeat steps 4-10 until no more jobs can be scheduled at current time
Step 12: Advance time to next event (minimum completion time among active jobs)
Step 13: Update resource availability by releasing resources from completed jobs
Step 14: Remove completed jobs from active job list
Step 15: Repeat steps 4-14 until all jobs are scheduled
Step 16: Calculate makespan as maximum completion time among all scheduled jobs
